referenced_message.content: First, figure out what do you want to test in the first place and what is the goal for that testing. What are you trying to find. If you want to test e.g. decompression speed, you need to do tests that don't touch filesystem, load stuff in memory, do decompression purely in memory, drop result.

content: I know the pain of using something that is not common (pin-modding, using special versions of windows and etc...).

Imho if we want to pick some other algorithms and have a good argument for it (I believe nowadays it's mostly about speed of decompression), it really comes down to zstd and lz4. Those are very widely used, and there is value in using stuff that's widely used, I would prefer not to use something that theoretically is slightly better but is more obscure.

First, figure out what do you want to test in the first place and what is the goal for that testing. What are you trying to find. If you want to test e.g. decompression speed, you need to do tests that don't touch filesystem, load stuff in memory, do decompression purely in memory, drop result.

though with thousands of small files we're also bottlenecked by the number of IO calls
Well, lzma2 at least is supposed to be gated by the CPU speed
I'm not sure how i can do it without real hardware bottleneck (NVME) interference

content: Can you also measure the decompression speed?

No clue if helpful, but...
I made fresh install of BAR and then i unpacked all filed with 7z ZS and packed it with maximum compression (with solid compression). Result:
we are already wasting players disk space by not having any garbage collection for old game versions, old maps etc
also disk space is getting still cheaper, its barely matters really to have the best possible compression ratio on disk
It's still awful AKU, no much changes, lzma2 decompression is still in order of ~60MB/s, gzip is ~250MB/s, zstd is ~600MB/s, and lz4 over 2000MB/s
But at same time we have more speedy RAM, more cores, faster cache...
SSE2 max
We target 15 years old processors in Recoil
Also it's was tested on haswell, it's was quite ago.
though lzma2 is supposed to have faster decompression than lzma
Everything is done before us https://indico.fnal.gov/event/16264/contributions/36466/attachments/22610/28037/Zstd__LZ4.pdf

embeds.title: Multithreaded Game Loading · Issue #1210 · beyond-all-reason/spring
embeds.description: Goals Speed up load times. Requirements No changes to the engine Lua API. Engine should be a drop in replacement for both BAR and Zero-K. Major Concerns Lua is not multi-thread safe. Open GL calls ...

content: Even though, theoretically FS performance might get us further (but maybe not due to lobby pre-caching).

From my perspective there are two main issues to tackle: FS performance, and running independent load routines concurrently. I'm not going to touch FS performance, but mostly focus on identifying independent load routines and running them concurrently.
For the Mt loading: if you do parallel file reads that helps on windows, but, e.g. engine rapid impl has some useless locking in place that kills it.

Ok did some profiling of LoadFile:

On Windows: 
- Files uncached: About 42% of load time spent in LoadFile (17 / 40 s)
- Files cached:   About 18% of load time spent in LoadFile (4 / 22s)

On Linux
- Files uncached: About 21% of load time spent in LoadFile (3.5 / 16 s)
- Files cached:   About 18% of load time spent in LoadFile (2.7 / 15 s)

Yeah, the only thing missing there is the optimization to hashing function we use: on every load every file is hashed with sha512, which is rather slow, adding ~2s on my bench. Changing to some quicker hash like blake3 might improve it.
Interesting - that does address FS performance directly.
Have you seen <https://github.com/beyond-all-reason/spring/issues/1183> I opened?

Also re packaging: we could abuse the `cache` directory to get some gains on loading on repeated loads. VFS already uses the `cache` dir to avoid re-scanning archives every startup, we can utilize it more.
Map definitely could be, all BAR ones are compressed with lzma, unpacking that in separate thread for some of the maps that are like 300MiB+ nowadays would be a win

Yes, I get it. I'm concerned with a few things:
0) IO might be the bottleneck, nothing can be done about it except better packaging
1) You can't really go parallel with different Lua environments, because each env can cross call the other environment.
2) LuaUI, unsynced LuaRules/LuaGaia can do GL --> must execute on GL enabled thread

referenced_message.content: Yep, that's what I"m trying to communicate in the `Limit` subheading of the initial issue report. Without changing any APIs for various games, the fastest loading sequence is to put the heavy lua scripts on the main thread, while offloading almost everything else on auxiliary loading thread, ensuring that the auxiliary loading threads gets assets ready as soon as the main thread needs it.

Yep, that's what I"m trying to communicate in the `Limit` subheading of the initial issue report. Without changing any APIs for various games, the fastest loading sequence is to put the heavy lua scripts on the main thread, while offloading almost everything else on auxiliary loading thread, ensuring that the auxiliary loading threads gets assets ready as soon as the main thread needs it.
I think your LoadFile benchmark might be incorrect btw
Moreover, at least in BAR, some auxiliary Lua scripts are time consuming which we can 100% offload (CExplosionGenerator::Init, Radar icons)
Unsynced Lua (LuaUI/LuaRules) will make a lot of GL calls, so can't put it parallel to somehow bulky WorldDrawer::Init

There are two 'main' environments in Lua scripts, before and after LuaRules are loaded. LuaIntro and LuaMenu both have limited enough access that some of the time consuming loading process can be done while LuaIntro and LuaMenu scripts are executing.
Yep, right
Since Lua is inherently single threaded and we don't want to touch that, we should be able to do a lot of work while the game lua scripts are executing.
Yeah but you can't MT with Lua too
A lot of time is spent executing Lua scripts - this is where our opportunity to improve load times come from.

(BAR)
